coredata.dc:title: Carbon dots bridging interface engineering of PVDF-HFP/ZnO nanoarrays cross-linked porous membranes for wastewater purification
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 Elsevier Ltd. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier Ltd. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Huang, Ping
Wang, Mingda
Xu, Shunjian
Liu, Lei
Fu, Haiyan
Zhong, Wei
Fan, Jirui
Xue, Wenxiang
Xiao, Zonghu
Huang, Jianhua
coredata.dc:description: Developing an efficient inorganic-organic composite material is a feasible strategy for flexible high-performance piezoelectric applications, but usually with poor interfacial compatibility. In this study, carbon dots (CDs) were tactfully adopted to bridge the interfaces of PVDF-HFP/ZnO nanoarrays through their rich surface functional groups. The "bridging effect" of CDs synergistically triggered the "induced polarization effect" for PVDF-HFP with β-phase formation of 86.9 %, and "interfacial stress transfer effect" between the matrix and the fillers with significantly enhanced binding energy, which made the piezoelectric coefficient improved to 37.74 pC N−1. Owing to the cross-linked porous structure of the membranes, there existed significant adsorption during the degradation process, in addition to ultrasonic vibration and piezocatalysis. Further, the degradation contributions of these three aspect were firstly deeply discussed. Based on the above material design, the ZnO@CDs/PVDF-HFP membranes exhibited a 70 % improvement of degradation rate when purifying organic dye wastewater. Moreover, the membranes had also demonstrated broad applications in some real-world wastewater from the textile industry and domestic life.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Interface engineering
Piezoelectric composite membranes
Wastewater purification

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Morphological and structural characteristics of the composite membranes
Piezoelectric properties and potential distributions of the composite membranes
Piezocatalysis application of the composite membranes for wastewater purification

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Preparation process and applications of the piezoelectric composite membranes.

(a) Surface and (b) cross-sectional SEM images of ZnO NAs@CDs, (c) TEM image and (d) FT-IR spectrum of CDs, (e,f) TEM image and (g) EDS spectra of ZnO NAs@CDs, (h) surface and (i) cross-sectional SEM images of the P-C-Z membrane.
(a) XRD patterns, (b) FT-IR spectra and (c) DTA heating curves of different membranes; (d) IGM color contour map of ZnO NAs/PVDF-HFP and ZnO NAs@CDs/PVDF-HFP.

(a) Applied force-voltage curves and (b) d33 of different membranes; COMSOL simulation: (c) real-time surface charge, (d) real-time output current, (e) real-time output voltage, (f) the maximum potential of different membranes, potential distribution of the P-C-Z membrane at (g) 0.5625 s and (h) 0.7625 s.

(a) The relationship between adsorption capacity and thickness of the membranes, (b) degradation rate of dye wastewater without membranes under ultrasound treatment, (c) the final degradation rate after 30 min, (d) degradation contributions of the membranes for MV, (e) piezocatalytic degradation contributions from the reactive species, (f) degradation contributions of the membranes for MB.
Enhanced piezocatalytic mechanism of the P-C-Z membrane.

head.ce:title: Carbon dots bridging interface engineering of PVDF-HFP/ZnO nanoarrays cross-linked porous membranes for wastewater purification

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: Ping Huang and Mingda Wang contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Developing an efficient inorganic-organic composite material is a feasible strategy for flexible high-performance piezoelectric applications, but usually with poor interfacial compatibility. In this study, carbon dots (CDs) were tactfully adopted to bridge the interfaces of PVDF-HFP/ZnO nanoarrays through their rich surface functional groups. The "bridging effect" of CDs synergistically triggered the "induced polarization effect" for PVDF-HFP with β-phase formation of 86.9 %, and "interfacial stress transfer effect" between the matrix and the fillers with significantly enhanced binding energy, which made the piezoelectric coefficient improved to 37.74 pC N−1. Owing to the cross-linked porous structure of the membranes, there existed significant adsorption during the degradation process, in addition to ultrasonic vibration and piezocatalysis. Further, the degradation contributions of these three aspect were firstly deeply discussed. Based on the above material design, the ZnO@CDs/PVDF-HFP membranes exhibited a 70 % improvement of degradation rate when purifying organic dye wastewater. Moreover, the membranes had also demonstrated broad applications in some real-world wastewater from the textile industry and domestic life.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Ordered carbon dots bridging interface engineering strengthened the piezoelectric effect of PVDF-HFP/ZnO nanoarrays cross-linked porous membranes for wastewater purification.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: The development of alternative clean energy technologies has attracted extensive research interest in addressing environmental pollution and energy shortage. Vibration energy from water flow, sound waves, wind and human movement is as widespread in nature as light energy, and can be collected and converted into electricity by the "piezoelectric effect" for energy harvesting, self-powered sensing and piezocatalysis, etc. [1–3] The emerging piezocatalysis makes advantage of this effect to form a piezoelectric potential to drive the generation of reactive species for organic pollutant degradation, sterilization and water splitting. [4–6] Up to now, a variety of inorganic piezoelectric materials (such as BaTiO3, MoS2 and ZnO, etc.) have been reported as self-powered sensors and piezocatalysts [7–9], but with some problems, such as poor flexibility, easy agglomeration, bad biocompatibility and difficult recovery, so they have to be embedded in certain matrix in engineering applications. Developing an efficient inorganic-organic composite membrane is a feasible strategy to solve the problem. PVDF-based materials (including derivatives PVDF-TrFE and PVDF-HFP, etc) are outstanding piezoelectric polymers due to their high electromechanical coupling, good biocompatibility and wide response range. ZnO/PVDF composite material is a rising star with its theoretical prediction of piezoelectric constant (d33) reaching 400 pC N−1, which is close to traditional piezoelectric ceramic materials. [10,11] However, with the prior art, the actual d33 value of ZnO/PVDF is far behind the theoretical value, which weakens its piezoelectric performance. The main reasons are that: (i) ZnO lacks functional groups on its surface and is prone to aggregation in the matrix, resulting in poor induced polarization effect on PVDF; and (ii) the weak interface bonding between ZnO and PVDF leads to ineffective stress transfer from the outside. [12,13]

To further improve the piezoelectric effect of ZnO/PVDF composites, the challenging task is to synergistically enhance the "induced polarization effect" and "interfacial stress transfer effect". The induced polarization effect governs the formation of the piezoelectric phase in PVDF. Allowing more surface states to ZnO, such as surface functional groups, dangling bonds and surface charges, is an effective method to further enhance the induced polarization effect. [14–16] On the other hand, Marmorejo Tejada et al. [17] proposed that the enhancement of stress distribution at the two-phase interface would greatly improve the piezoelectric properties of composite materials, and speculated that the enhanced stress distribution was due to the dispersion force between ZnO and PVDF molecular chains, which promoted the hardening effect at the interface. Our research group has ever sensitized carbon dots (CDs) onto the surface of ZnO nanoparticles, and then embedded them as dispersed nanoparticles into the PVDF-HFP matrix. Consequently, the β phase content and d33 of the composite membranes increased by 22.4 % and 71.6 % respectively, and the antibacterial rate against Staphylococcus aureus reached 69.1 %. [18] CDs can be regarded as core-shell materials, with amorphous or sp2-sp3 hybrid crystals as the central carbon nucleus. The spherical shell is rich in defects and functional groups, and the types of surface functional groups are abundant and can be regulated. [19,20] These surface functional groups provide active sites for the strong chemical bonding between ZnO and PVDF-HFP, which to a large extent make up for the lack of surface functional groups and weak interface bonding of ZnO. However, the intrinsic piezoelectric effect of ZnO is mainly concentrated in the c-axis, and the random dispersed nanoparticles cannot exert this effect effectively. The ZnO nanoarrays (NAs) themselves have good dispersion, which can completely avoid the agglomeration problem of fillers, and their high orientation can give full play to the intrinsic piezoelectric effect.

Among PVDF-based polymers, PVDF-HFP with moderate price has superior thermal stability, chemical corrosion resistance and mechanical properties, making it with broad application prospects in fields such as battery separators, ion exchange membranes, and water purification treatment membranes. Therefore, we choose the PVDF-HFP polymer for energy harvesting applications, especially in the field of water purification treatment. Encouraged by the above new developments, this study adopts CDs to bridge the ZnO NAs/PVDF-HFP interface to synergistically enhance the "induced polarization effect" and "interface stress transfer effect". In terms of interface design, CDs and ZnO form a unique core-shell "bridge ring" with strong interface combination (ZnO@CDs), and the surface functional groups and morphology of the hybrid fillers can be widely regulated. The rich surface functional groups located in the shell can not only promote the formation of β phases by increasing the induced active points, but also improve the strong bonding between the filler and the matrix to enhance the interface bonding and stress transfer, thus improving the piezoelectric coefficient by 122.5 %. Owing to the cross-linked porous structure of the membranes, there existed significant adsorption during the degradation process, in addition to ultrasonic vibration and piezocatalysis. Finally, the ZnO@CDs/PVDF-HFP membranes were catalytically used to decompose organic dyes with a 70 % improvement of degradation rate, compared with the pure PVDF-HFP membrane. Furthermore, the enhanced piezoelectric performance and entire degradation process were deeply discussed.

Firstly, ZnO nanoparticles synthesized through a convenient hydrolysis reaction [21], were covered on the ITO-PEN substrate (15 mm × 15 mm) for nucleation. Secondly, the ZnO nanoparticles-coated ITO substrate was floated upside down in an nutrient solution, consisting of zinc acetate dihydrate (0.01 M) and hexamethylenetetramine (0.01 M). Then the ZnO NAs were hydrothermally grown at the temperature of 90 °C for 8 h.

The whole preparation process of piezocatalytic composite membranes was showed in Fig. 1. Firstly, the CDs prepared by the previous reported method [18], were coated around ZnO NAs to get ZnO NAs@CDs by immersing ZnO NAs into the CDs solution for 24 h. Secondly, PVDF-HFP (0.6 g), DMF (3 mL) and acetone (2 mL) were stirred magnetically for 2 h to obtain a homogeneous solution. Thirdly, the PVDF-HFP solution was spin-coated on the ZnO NAs@CDs-grown ITO substrate at the rotating speed of 1000 rpm for 60 s. Then place the membrane in a certain environment (60 % RH, 30 °C) for half an hour to form a cross-linked porous structure through vapor-induced phase separation (VIPS). Lastly, the obtained membrane was annealed at the temperature of 120 °C for 1 h, marked as P-C-Z. At the same time, pure PVDF-HFP membrane and PVDF-HFP/ZnO membrane were also fabricated by the above method for comparison, marked as P-0–0 and P-0-Z, respectively. The piezocatalytic composite membranes was used for degrading organic dye wastewater in this study.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM, JEM-2100, JEOL) and scanning electron microscope (SEM, EVO MA10, Zeiss) were used to observe the morphologies of the nanoarrays and membranes. The structural properties of the membranes were investigated by Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR, Nicolet IS50, Thermo Scientific), X-ray diffraction (XRD, D8 advance, Bruker) and differential thermal analysis (DTA, STA2500, NETZSCH). The piezoelectric properties of the devices were explored by an electromechanical platform, comprising a stepper motor (FSL40, Sichuan Fuyu), a commercial mechanical sensor (M6x1, Anhui Qisheng), an oscilloscope (UTD7102BG, Guangdong UNI-T) and a charge-voltage converter (VK102, Guangdong Weijingyi). The absorption spectra of the dye solution were recorded by the ultraviolet-visible spectrophotometer (UV-Vis, TU-1901, Beijing Puxi).

Density functional theory (DFT) calculations using the Vienna Ab-initio Simulation Package (VASP) [22] were employed to investigate the structural optimization and binding energy of the system. The projector augmented wave (PAW) potentials [23] with Perdew–Burke–Ernzerhof (PBE) functional [24] were utilized. A kinetic energy cutoff of 550 eV was used for wave function expansion. To include van der Waals interactions, the DFT-D3 method was employed. The equilibrium geometries were obtained when the atomic forces were less than 0.010 eV/Å. Periodic boundary conditions were employed, and a 3 × 3 × 3 k-point mesh was used for structure optimization. For self-consistent charge density calculations, a 6 × 6 × 6 k-point mesh was employed. In this molecular structure calculations, we included sufficiently large vacuum regions (≥ 15 Å) to prevent interactions between periodic images during molecular structure optimization. Subsequently, the crystal cell was optimized to obtain the corresponding crystal structure. The binding energy was determined as the energy difference between the crystal and the isolated molecules. The intermolecular interaction forces were qualitatively analyzed through the Independent Gradient Model (IGM) [25,26] through of Multiwfn software [27].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Morphological and structural characteristics of the composite membranes
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 2(a,b) showed the surface and cross-sectional SEM images of ZnO NAs@CDs, indicating that ZnO NAs grew vertically on the substrate evenly with the length of about 5.2 μm. For comparison, the morphologies of ZnO NAs without sensitizing CDs were exhibited in Fig. S1, which didn’t show much difference from ZnO NAs@CDs. In order to further investigate the state of CDs on the ZnO NAs surface, TEM tests were conducted. Fig. 2(c) presented the TEM morphology of the as-prepared CDs, which was quasi-spherically crystalline with an average particle of 8.5 nm. The FT-IR spectrum of CDs from Fig. 2(d) indicated the CDs were rich in several surface functional groups: –OH and –NH2 (3420 cm–1 and 3200 cm–1), –COOH (1700 cm–1), CC and CN (1620 cm–1), etc. From the cross-sectional TEM images of ZnO NAs@CDs in Fig. 2(e), it was distinctly observed that mono-layered CDs were orderly covered over the entire surface of the ZnO NAs. In detail, the central zone between the two blue line was the ZnO NAs core with a diameter of about 33 nm, while the region between the adjacent blue and red line was the CDs shell. On the whole, the ZnO NAs core and the CDs shell formed the ZnO NAs@CDs core-shell structure. The enlarged image in Fig. 2(f) clearly displayed the CDs particles connected on the ZnO surface, with the dimension similar to Fig. 2(c). Additionally, the EDS spectra of ZnO NAs@CDs in Fig. 2(g) also confirmed the formation of the composite material, due to the existence of Zn, O and C elements.

After spin-coating the PVDF-HFP solution into ZnO NAs@CDs, the solvent was extracted out from the solution by the water vapor in the environment through VIPS. [28] Finally, a uniform cross-linked porous piezoelectric membrane was obtained with the average pore size of 1.93 ± 0.36 and the thickness of about 19.5 μm, as shown in Fig. 2(h,i) and Fig. S3(c). Correspondingly, Fig. S2 exhibited the SEM images of P-0–0 and P-0-Z membranes, indicating that the piezoelectric membranes all formed uniform cross-linked porous structures of similar size, specifically 1.99 ± 0.39 μm for the P-0-Z membrane and 1.82 ± 0.40 μm for the P-0–0 membrane (Fig. S3(a,b)). However, the thickness of the piezoelectric membranes showed a certain difference, specifically 18.0 μm for the P-0-Z membrane and 16.5 μm for the P-0–0 membrane. This was mainly because ZnO NAs and ZnO NAs@CDs exerted adhesion to PVDF-HFP, resulting in more PVDF-HFP remaining on the substrate during the spin-coating process. Moreover, the latter had greater adhesion than the former. Further, cross-sectional SEM images of the membranes have been provided to help understand the changes as shown in Fig. S4. Due to the complete coverage of PVDF-HFP on the surface of the fillers, the fillers themselves could not be observed, but the fillers caused significant changes in the pore shape of the membrane. In the direction of membrane thickness, PVDF-HFP formed vertical lines along the arrays of the fillers (Fig. S4(b)), while the pure PVDF-HFP membrane only had some disordered pores (Fig. S4(d)). It was worth mentioning that the introduction of the developed pore structure in piezoelectric materials might bring multiple benefits in terms of material properties. On one hand, the increase of porosity can improve the piezoelectric voltage coefficient, thus improving the piezoelectric and piezocatalytic performance. [1,29] On the other hand, in addition to piezocatalytic degradation, cross-linked porous piezocatalytic membranes can form adsorption effects on organic pollutants to assist in wastewater purification.

The piezoelectric properties of PVDF-HFP are strongly related to the phase structure, especially the β phase content with all-trans structure. Therefore, the crystal structure characteristics of the composite membranes were first investigated. From the XRD patterns of the composite membranes in Fig. 3(a), there existed three peaks associated with the PVDF-HFP phase structure, including 20.8°, 19.1° and 40.6°. The first peak was attributed to the β phase of (200) (110) crystal plane, while the remaining corresponded to the α phase (020) and (002) crystal planes respectively. [30] The additional peaks at 32.1°, 35.0° and 36.9° of the P-0-Z and P-C-Z membranes were ascribed to the (100), (002) and (101) crystal planes of ZnO respectively [31], proving the embedding of ZnO NAs and ZnO NAs@CDs in the composite membranes. Moreover, the intensity ratio of β (20.8°)/α (19.1°) of the membranes increased with the ZnO NAs and ZnO NAs@CDs embedding, especially the P-C-Z membrane reached the maximum. The above analysis indicated that ZnO could induce the formation of β phase in PVDF-HFP, and the modification of ZnO surface by CDs was beneficial to further enhance this effect. FT-IR analysis was generally used to further quantify the phase structure composition of the prepared piezoelectric membranes. As shown in Fig. 3(b), the characteristic absorption bands at 512 cm−1, 841 cm−1 and 1277 cm−1 demonstrated the existence of β phase in PVDF-HFP, while the peaks at 608 cm−1 and 766 cm−1 were ascribed to α phase of PVDF-HFP. [32] Assuming that FT-IR absorption obeyed the Lambert-Beer law, the relative content of β phase (F(β)) in PVDF-HFP including only α and β phases could be calculated by Eq. 1: [33]
(1)
F ( β ) =
1
1 +
k
β
k
α
×
A
α
A
β
where A and k were the absorption intensity and the absorption coefficient of a certain phase, respectively. Particularly, the peaks at 766 cm−1 and 841 cm−1 were generally represented as α and β phases here, and the absorption coefficient of the corresponding peaks were 6.1 × 104 cm2 mol−1 and 7.7 × 104 cm2 mol−1, respectively. To guarantee the validity of the experimental results, five samples were prepared for each group, and the measured β-phase content were shown in Table S1. Hence, the average β-phase content of the P-0–0, P-0-Z and P-C-Z membranes were calculated to be 70.7 ± 4.1 %, 79.3 ± 1.7 % and 86.9 ± 3.0 %, respectively. In addition, the crystallization behavior of the piezoelectric materials was studied by using DTA as a supplementary technique. It was found from the DTA heating curves in Fig. 3(c) that the melting temperature of the membranes tended to be higher with the doping of ZnO NAs and ZnO NAs@CDs. Specifically, the embedding of ZnO NAs facilitated PVDF-HFP to form ordered chains parallel to ZnO NAs, thus improving the crystallinity of PVDF-HFP. In particular, the induced crystallization was further enhanced when the surface of ZnO NAs were coated with CDs rich in functional groups. Based on the above analysis of the crystal structure characteristics of the composite membranes, it could be concluded that the embedding of ZnO NAs was conducive to improving the crystallinity of PVDF-HFP, especially increasing the β phase content of PVDF-HFP. Moreover, the above effect was further strengthened when the ZnO/PVDF-HFP interfaces were bridged with CDs, which would strongly promote its piezoelectric properties.

In order to confirm the theoretical feasibility of CDs bridging ZnO/PVDF-HFP interface, DFT calculations using VASP were employed to investigate the structural optimization and binding energy of the system. The optimized structures of ZnO@CDs/PVDF-HFP and ZnO/PVDF-HFP were obtained through structural calculations, as shown in Fig. S5. Through a comparative analysis of the binding energies of ZnO NAs@CDs/PVDF-HFP and ZnO NAs/PVDF-HFP, the addition of CDs significantly enhanced the system's binding energy. In detail, the binding energy of the ZnO NAs/PVDF-HFP system was only −2.32 eV, while the binding energies ZnO NAs/CDs and CDs/PVDF-HFP were found to be −10.02 eV and −5.11 eV, respectively. IGM analysis from Fig. 3(d) revealed that the increase in binding energy was primarily attributed to the enhanced binding capabilities of surface functional groups (especially carboxyl and hydroxyl groups) in CDs with Zn and O in ZnO, as well as with F in PVDF-HFP. The improved interfacial binding capability of the system could effectively realize the interface stress transfer between the matrix material PVDF-HFP and the filling material ZnO NAs/CDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Piezoelectric properties and potential distributions of the composite membranes
ce:section.ce:para: To investigate the piezoelectric properties of the composite membranes, simple piezoelectric devices were prepared using the sandwich structure. Fig. S6 showed the real-time output voltages of the P-0–0, P-0-Z and P-C-Z piezoelectric membranes under some different repeating forces. A common feature was that the output voltage of piezoelectric membranes increased with the augment of applied force, which was consistent with the piezoelectric effect. To further quantify the relationship between the two, the force applied to the membranes and its corresponding forward output voltage were obtained to constitute the applied force-voltage curves shown in Fig. 4(a). It was obvious that there was a good linear relationship (U = a + b * F) between the output voltage (U) and the applied force (F) for all these three membranes with R2 ≥ 0.994 in the wide range from 0 to 50 N, which indicated the potential to be used as stress sensors with high stress tolerance. The detailed parameters of all the membranes were exhibited in Table S2. The voltage sensitivity of the membrane could be defined using the slope of the line (b = ΔU / ΔF). Thereby, the voltage sensitivities of P-0–0, P-0-Z and P-C-Z membranes were 16.96 mV N−1, 29.92 mV N−1 and 37.74 mV N−1, respectively. The highest voltage sensitivity for the P-C-Z membrane was owing to the piezoelectric properties of the P-C-Z membrane. To test the durability of the device, repeatedly dynamic load of about 30 N were applied to the P-C-Z sensor for over 1000 cycles. As seen form Fig. S7, the output voltage of the device didn’t have much change, indicating that the device had adequate mechanical and electrical stability as a stress sensor.

Further, the piezoelectric coefficient (d33) was commonly used to characterize the piezoelectric properties of materials. The charge (Q) generated across the opposite faces of the piezoelectric material was directly proportional to the applied force (Q = d33 * F) [34], and the ratio was exactly d33. The voltage output from the charge amplifier had a direct proportional relationship with the surface charge of the piezoelectric material (Q = k * U, where k = 1000 pC V−1). Therefore, d33 could be calculated according to the corresponding voltage value under the applied force (d33 = k * U / F). In this study, d33 of the P-0–0, P-0-Z and P-C-Z membranes were calculated as 16.96 pC N−1, 29.92 pC N−1 and 37.74 pC N−1 respectively, as shown in Fig. 4(b). The amplitude-bias voltage curves of the membranes were measured to calculate converse piezoelectric coefficient (g33) according to Eq. 2,
(2)
g
33
=
A −
A
0
V −
V
0
where A and V represented the amplitude of the membrane and the tip bias voltage, and (A0, V0) was the intersection of the curve. As calculated from Fig. S8, their g33 values were about 24.4 pm V−1, 31.7 pm V−1 and 38.8 pm V−1, which were very close to the direct piezoelectric coefficient (d33).

Moreover, ferroelectric studies were carried out to confirm the enhancement of piezoelectric performance. Ferroelectric hysteresis loops of the porous PVDF-HFP based membranes were obtained at 1 Hz under the maximum field amplitude of 100 MV m−1 as exhibited in Fig. S9. These slender curves indicated that these membranes have the characteristics of low loss and high energy conversion efficiency, making them suitable for sensing and energy harvesting. The remnant polarization of P-0–0, P-0-Z, and P-Z-C were 0.39 μC cm−2, 0.65 μC cm−2 and 0.82 μC cm−2, respectively. After the addition of the ZnO fillers, the interface polarization formed between the fillers and the PVDF-HFP matrix would enhance the residual polarization of the membrane. When CDs further modified the interface between ZnO and PVDF-HFP, the binding energy of the entire system was significantly increased as calculated from DFT in Fig. S5, thereby further improving the residual polarization of the material.

Dielectric properties are important for a piezoelectric nanocomposite as these properties are interrelated. The relative dielectric constant and dielectric loss of these were measured with the frequency ranging from 102 to 106 Hz as shown in Fig. S10. The enhanced interfacial polarization of the composite membrane was also an important factor in improving the dielectric constant of the composite. Due to the interface adjustment to the carrier transmission, the conductive paths might be optimized to lower dielectric loss. Especially after modifying the surface of ZnO with CDs, more β phase with spontaneous polarization effect in PVDF-HFP was induced, which was beneficial for improving dielectric properties. In order to demonstrate the influence of introduced pores on the dielectric constant of the membrane and eliminate the interference of additives, a dense PVDF-HFP membrane was prepared, and its measured dielectric constant was compared with that of the porous PVDF-HFP membrane, as exhibited in Fig. S11. Specifically, the dense membrane was obtained by isolating water molecules after spin coating to prevent the vapor induced phase separation. It was found that the dielectric constant of the membrane decreased due to the introduction of pores.

To verify the effectiveness of piezoelectric properties of the composite membranes, the piezoelectric parameters under the applied force were calculated via finite element method simulation using COMSOL Multiphysics software [11,35,36]. Fig. S12(a) exhibited the configure of the piezoelectric materials, and the actual applied force on these materials was given as Fig. S12(b). The calculated surface charge, output current and output voltage of the composite membranes under the applied force were shown in Fig. 4(c), Fig. 4(d) and Fig. 4(e), respectively. It could be found that the P-C-Z membrane had the best piezoelectric performance, followed by the P-0-Z membrane and finally the P-0–0 membrane. The detailed calculated values in Fig. 4(f) were in good agreement with the experimental results in Fig. 4(a). To better display the potential distribution in the membrane, a end face of the membrane was chosen as a zero potential surface. Fig. 4(g) and Fig. 4(h) showed the three-dimensional potential distributions of the P-C-Z membrane at 0.5625 s and 0.7625 s, respectively. (The elasticity matrix and coupling matrix of the material were provided in Note 1 of Supporting Information) It should be noted that these two time points corresponded to the maximum positive and negative output voltages of the membrane in Fig. 4(e), respectively. Thus, the potential difference between the upper and lower surfaces of the membrane was exactly equal to the output voltage. For comparison, the potential distributions of the P-0-Z membrane and the P-0–0 membrane were exhibited in Fig. S12(c,d) and Fig. S12(e,f), respectively. It could be clearly seen that, in the direction of the thickness of the membrane, the potential distribution was unidirectional, and the direction of increase was different at different times. Specially, the potential distribution of the membrane increased unidirectionally from the lower surface to the upper surface at 0.5625 s, while it is just the opposite at 0.7625 s.

The improved piezoelectric effect of the composite membrane could be contributed to three main aspects involved. (1) The intrinsic piezoelectric effect of embedded ZnO NAs, without agglomeration problem, could play a synergistic role with PVDF-HFP. However, there also existed issues of the obstructed stress transfer through the incompatibility interface and the insufficient induced active points. (2) CDs sensitized on the surface of ZnO NAs could just solve the above problems, owing to the rich surface functional groups of CDs, acting as "bridge ring" with strong interface combination between ZnO NAs and PVDF-HFP (as demonstrated in Fig. 2 and Fig. 3). (3) Besides, CDs exerted the "induced polarization effect" on the β-phase formation of PVDF-HFP by increasing the induced active points, thus improving the piezoelectric coefficient of the composite materials (as verified from Fig. 3). The improved piezoelectric effect of the composite membrane would strengthen the application of the composite membrane in wastewater purification.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Piezocatalysis application of the composite membranes for wastewater purification
ce:section.ce:para: The above experiment and simulation results confirmed that the piezoelectric properties of the composite membranes were greatly improved by the bridging effect of CDs on the PVDF-HFP/ZnO interfaces, so we tried to use these composite films for wastewater purification. In the following study, the degradation mechanism of dye wastewater by the piezoelectric composite membranes under ultrasonic vibration would be investigated. Methyl violet (MV) dye and methylene blue (MB) dye (10 mg L−1) were chosen as the degradation targets to evaluate the piezocatalytic performance of the composite membranes under the ultrasonic vibration excitation with the power of 100 W and frequency of 40 kHz. Before the ultrasonic degradation of organic dyes, the composite membranes were soaking in the dye solutions for 30 min to reach the adsorption-desorption equilibrium. Every 5 min of ultrasonic treatment, the sample solution was taken once to test the absorption spectrum. Fig. S13 showed the absorption spectra of MV after membrane immersion and ultrasonic excitation with the existence of the piezoelectric membranes. Obviously, it was found that the absorbance of MV solution decreased significantly after the membrane was soaked in it, revealing that all the membranes had a significant adsorption effect on dyes. This phenomenon was different from what had been reported in most reports, but it matched the structure of the cross-linked porous membranes in this study. Fig. S14 presented the macroscopic morphologies of the membranes after soaking in solution. In addition, with the extension of ultrasonic time, MV was continuously decomposed and the solution concentration degraded. To further quantify the results, the absorbance of MV at the maximum absorption wavelength of 575 nm was extracted to calculate the degradation rate as Fig. S15. There were certain differences in the adsorption capacity of these three membranes, with the P-C-Z membrane being the largest (13.4 %), followed by the P-0-Z membrane (11.9 %) and the P-0–0 membrane (9.3 %). Because the ambient temperature and humidity of the VIPS process in this study were the same, the pore structure were almost the same. So the absorption differences of the membranes might be positively correlated with the membrane thickness as Fig. 5(a). After ultrasound treatment for 30 min, the final degradation rates of the P-0–0, P-0-Z and P-C-Z membranes on MV were 28.1 %, 36.8 % and 46.6 %, respectively.

In addition to adsorption, several other controlled experiments were designed to further explore the mechanism of dye degradation, as shown in Fig. S16. First of all, without any piezoelectric membrane, only the wastewater itself was treated by ultrasound. The results from Fig. 5(b) revealed that ultrasound had a certain degradation effect on MV, and ultrasound treatment of 30 min could degrade MV by 8.4 %. Then radical quenching experiments with different selective scavengers were carried out to distinguish the dominating reactive species, with the P-C-Z membrane as the experimental representative. Benzoquinone (BQ), tertiary butyl alcohol (TBA) and ethylene diamine tetraacetic acid (EDTA) acted as the scavenger of ·O2 -, ·OH and h+, respectively. [37,38] It was found from Fig. 5(c) that all these three reactive species contributed to the piezocatalytic degradation of MV, in which ·OH and h+ played a more important role. In addition to MV, the degradation effect of the composite membranes on MB was also studied. In combination with Fig. S17 and Fig. S18, the degradation performance of MB was relatively similar to that of MV. The difference was that EDTA exhibited a more prominent inhibitory effect on piezocatalytic degradation of MB. The generation process of reactive species might involve the following reactions: [39,40]
Piezocatalytic membrane + vibration → e- + h+
O2 + e- → ·O2 -
H2O + h+ → H+ + ·OH

Based on the above analysis, there were three synergistic effects in the entire degradation process of organic dyes. (1) The cross-linked porous composite membrane had adsorption effect on organic dyes, which was related to the membrane thickness and pore structure. Because the ambient temperature and humidity of the VIPS process in this study were the same, the pore structure were almost the same. So the adsorption difference was almost derived from the membrane thickness. (2) Due to the collapsed cavitation bubbles form the instantaneous high-density energy, ultrasonic vibration had a certain degradation effect on organic dyes, which may be related to the power and frequency of the ultrasound used. Nevertheless, these parameters of the ultrasound were fixed in this study. (3) The piezocatalysis of the composite membranes played a major role in the degradation of organic dyes, originating from the generation of reactive species such as ·O2 -, ·OH and h+, but with varying contributions. In the degradation process of MV for this study, the degradation contributions of the above three aspects were summarized in Table S3 and Fig. 5(d). Fig. 5(e) showed the piezocatalytic degradation contributions of each reactive specie for the P-C-Z membrane. It was noted that, in terms of MV, the sum of all the degradation contributions from the reactive species in Fig. 5(e) was greater than the total piezocatalytic degradation contribution in Fig. 5(d). This was because the generation process of some reactive species overlapped, such as OH- + h+ → ·OH. The situation of MB in Table S4 and Fig. 5(f) was similar. In general, piezocatalysis played the most important role in the degradation of dyes, but the adsorption of the membrane itself could not be ignored.

In view of the piezocatalytic performance of the P-C-Z membrane superior to other membranes, Fig. 6 proposed the enhancement mechanism. The improved piezoelectric effect of the P-C-Z composite membrane (discussed in 3.2) could adsorb more free charges from external environment and build larger internal electric field under the same stress. The evaluated built-in electric field facilitated the separation of electrons and holes in ZnO NAs and their transfer to the surfaces on both sides of the materials. All of these increased free charges, whether from the external environment or inside the material, would eventually interact with the contacted liquid to generation more reactive species. The yield of reactive species, as an intermediate between piezoelectric potential and catalytic performance, played a significant role in the research of piezocatalysis. In the future, we will focus on the yield of reactive species to establish the structure-activity relationship between interface regulation and piezocatalytic performance. In brief, the cross-linked porous piezocatalytic composite membranes with CDs bridging ZnO NAs/PVDF-HFP interfaces have the potential application for wastewater purification, such as the filter membrane of water purifier. Driven by the incoming water flow, the piezocatalysis formed by the filter membrane can degrade organic matter in water and even sterilize it.

The main performance parameters of the P-C-Z membranes were compared with other PVDF-based piezocatalysts for organic pollutant degradation as Table S5. In general, for such a thin piezocatalytic membrane, it exhibited comparatively good degradation performance under relatively mild catalytic conditions in a short time. As is well known that the degradation performance of the piezocatalytic membrane is closely related to the piezoelectric properties (piezoelectric coefficient and dielectric coefficient), dimension/weight, catalytic condition and substrate, etc. Currently, there are no standard testing conditions to limit the above related parameters, so it is often difficult to compare the degradation performance of piezoelectric films. We hereby suggest that the authorities can establish corresponding testing standards for the degradation performance of piezocatalytic membranes. Some real-world wastewater from the textile industry and domestic life were used to test the degradation capacity of the P-C-Z membranes. Following the same method as the previous degradation of dye wastewater, absorption spectra of the real-world wastewater were obtained after soaking and ultrasonic treatment, as exhibited in Fig. S19. After ultrasound treatment for 30 min, the final degradation rates of the membranes for industrial and domestic wastewater were 79.0 % and 51.1 %, respectively. It could be seen that the membranes had broad application prospects in the field of water purification.

In this study, CDs were used to bridge the interfaces of PVDF-HFP/ZnO NAs to prepare high-performance piezoelectric membranes. On one side, surface functional groups of CDs induced more β-phase formation of PVDF-HFP up to 86.9 %. On the other side, the "bridge ring" could assist the interfacial stress transfer between the matrix and the filling materials because of significantly enhanced binding energy. Eventually the composite membranes exhibited excellent piezoelectric performance with the d33 value improved to 37.74 pC N−1. The piezoelectric membranes were catalytically utilized in wastewater purification with increasing the degradation rate by 70 %. There were three synergistic effects in the entire degradation process of organic dyes: cross-linked porous adsorption, ultrasonic vibration and piezocatalysis. Among them, the role piezocatalysis played was the most important, owing to the reactive species generated by the outstanding piezoelectric membrane participating in the degradation of organic dyes, especially ·OH and h+.

Xiao Zonghu: Supervision, Resources. Xue Wenxiang: Investigation, Data curation. Fan Jirui: Investigation, Data curation. Liu Lei: Software, Investigation, Formal analysis. Xu Shunjian: Writing – review & editing, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization. Zhong Wei: Visualization, Investigation. Fu Haiyan: Validation, Formal analysis. Huang Jianhua: Validation. Wang Mingda: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Formal analysis. Huang Ping: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Formal analysis.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This research was financially supported by Natural Science Foundation of Jiangxi Province (20232BAB204010; 20224BAB214023), National Natural Science Foundation of China (52462008), Natural Science Foundation of Zhejiang Province (LY24E020007), Education Department of Jiangxi Province (GJJ212318; GJJ2202201) and Natural Science Foundation of Hunan Province (2022JJ60057), China.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.jece.2025.115635.
